worth_it_explanation: Programmatic deals carry negotiated demand at agreed prices, so a deal that stalls or that fights the open market costs revenue that the publisher already secured on paper. The check reads the deal setup and recent delivery a publisher already has in Equativ, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete deal that can be reactivated, repriced, or reprioritized. Because deal revenue is committed demand, catching a stalled or misaligned deal recovers money that was meant to be earned rather than chasing new demand.
intro: Programmatic deals route negotiated demand through Equativ at prices and terms the publisher agreed with a specific buyer, which is different from the open market where any buyer bids at the floor. This subcategory covers Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals running through the platform, both whether each deal is actually delivering and whether its price and priority sit correctly against the open auction. These checks confirm that the demand a publisher negotiated is reaching impressions rather than sitting idle, and that the deals and the open market are not undercutting each other.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored, so stalled deals go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A negotiated deal represents demand the publisher already secured, so a deal that stops delivering leaves committed revenue uncollected while no error surfaces. Without a regular read of deal IDs, spend, and impressions, a stalled or near-dead deal can sit unnoticed for weeks, which turns a fixable configuration or targeting mismatch into lost revenue across the whole flight.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal is an agreement between a publisher and a specific buyer to transact inventory at negotiated terms, identified by a deal ID that the buyer's bid carries through the auction. In Equativ, a Private Marketplace (PMP) deal invites a named buyer to bid on a defined slice of inventory at an agreed floor, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal commits the buyer to a fixed volume at a fixed price. Either way, the publisher has negotiated demand on the books, and the value of that demand only arrives if the deal actually delivers impressions.

Deals fail quietly. Unlike a broken tag or a missing pricing rule, a deal that stops spending throws no error. The buyer may have paused the campaign on their side, the targeting on the deal may no longer match available inventory, the deal ID may have been mistyped when it was set up, or the flight may have ended without anyone noticing the renewal lapsed. In each case the inventory keeps serving open-market demand, so revenue does not drop to zero and nothing looks broken, yet the higher-value committed demand the publisher negotiated is going unfilled.

Monitoring deal health means reading each active deal against what it was supposed to deliver. A healthy PMP deal shows bids and wins flowing at or above its floor across the flight. A healthy PG deal shows delivery pacing toward its committed volume. A deal that shows zero or near-zero impressions partway through its flight, or one that is winning far below its negotiated volume, points to a problem worth chasing while the flight is still live.

A few signs point to deal health going unmonitored:
  • No regular report breaks out spend and impressions by deal ID, so a stalled deal would not surface until a buyer complained or a flight closed short
  • Active deal IDs exist in the platform with no recent delivery, and no one can say whether that is expected or a fault
  • PG deals are tracked only by their end date rather than by pacing toward committed volume across the flight
You may want to set a regular read of delivery by deal ID so a stalled deal surfaces while it can still be fixed. One approach is to compare each active deal's recent impressions and spend against its expected pace, then investigate any deal sitting at or near zero before its flight ends.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, deals often attach to premium placements or sponsorship-adjacent sections that a buyer specifically negotiated, so a stalled deal there means a high-value slot is clearing open-market demand instead of the committed price. You may want to monitor deals on premium inventory most closely because that is where the gap between the negotiated price and the open-market fallback is widest.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target product or category sections where a buyer wants audience close to a purchase, and those deals can pace unevenly because the inventory volume shifts with seasonal traffic. You may want to read deal delivery against the actual available impressions on the targeted sections rather than a flat expected pace, so a seasonal dip is not mistaken for a stalled deal.

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed configuration and reporting documentation for deal monitoring is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each active deal ID against its recent delivery to see whether spend and impressions are flowing as expected or whether any deal has stalled.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the deals section of the Equativ console and list the active Private Marketplace (PMP) and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals with their deal IDs, buyers, floors, and flight dates.
For each deal, note whether it shows recent bids, wins, and impressions, or whether it sits at or near zero partway through its flight.
For PG deals, record the committed volume and compare delivery so far against the pace needed to meet it by the end date.
Flag any deal with active status but no recent delivery, and record the deal ID, buyer, and flight window for follow-up.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID with impressions, spend, and average price across the current flight.
Compare each deal's delivery against its expected pace or committed volume to see which deals are on track and which are stalled.
Note any deal whose recent impressions dropped to or near zero while its flight is still live, which points to a paused campaign, a targeting mismatch, or a setup fault.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active deal, whether recent spend and impressions are flowing in line with the deal's expected pace or committed volume. A set of deals where each active deal is delivering as expected, or where any gap has a recorded explanation, passes. An active deal sitting at or near zero impressions partway through its flight with no explanation, or a Programmatic Guaranteed deal pacing well short of its committed volume, is a fail recorded with the deal ID, buyer, and flight window affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active deal's recent impressions and spend against its expected pace or committed volume above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor deal health by deal ID, so a stalled Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal can sit unnoticed while its inventory clears lower-value open-market demand. Deals fail quietly because a deal that stops delivering throws no error, and the negotiated demand the publisher secured goes unfilled without revenue dropping to zero. {{voice_recommend}} setting a regular read of impressions and spend by deal ID, comparing each active deal against its expected pace or committed volume, and investigating any deal sitting at or near zero before its flight ends. After the change, keep the deal-level read on a regular cadence so a stall surfaces while it can still be fixed.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priority are not aligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor set below where the open market already clears lets a negotiated buyer win at a discount the publisher did not intend, and a deal priority set below the open auction lets a higher open-market bid take inventory the deal was meant to secure. Either misalignment moves revenue the wrong way, so the deal and the open market end up undercutting each other rather than working together.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal sits alongside the open auction, not outside it, so the price and the priority a publisher assigns to a deal decide how the deal and the open market interact. The deal floor is the minimum price the negotiated buyer pays. The deal's priority decides whether a deal bid is preferred over a higher open-market bid or whether the highest bid simply wins. When those two settings are aligned with what the open market is already doing, the deal captures the committed demand it was meant to. When they are not, the deal and the open auction work against each other.

Two misalignments cost money in opposite directions. If the deal floor is set below where the inventory already clears in the open market, the negotiated buyer wins at a discount the publisher never intended. The buyer is paying the deal floor while open-market bids were clearing higher. If the deal priority is set so the highest bid always wins and the deal carries no preference, a higher open-market bid can outbid the deal and take the impression, which defeats the point of negotiating committed demand for that inventory. In the language of header bidding, this is the difference between letting deals win on preference and letting price alone decide, and the prioritization preference has to be set deliberately for deals to behave the way the publisher negotiated.

Getting the alignment right means reading the deal floor against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory, and confirming the priority matches the intent of the deal. A Programmatic Guaranteed deal that commits volume usually needs priority above the open auction so the committed impressions are protected. A Private Marketplace deal meant to compete on price may sit at the same priority as the open market, in which case its floor needs to reflect real value rather than a number set once and forgotten.

A few signs point to deal floors or priority being misaligned:
  • A deal floor sits below the average price the same inventory clears in the open auction, so the deal buyer wins at a discount
  • A Programmatic Guaranteed deal is set at the same priority as the open market, so open-market bids can outbid the committed volume
  • Deal floors were set once at negotiation and have not been compared against current open-market clearing prices since
You may want to confirm each deal's floor and priority match both the negotiated intent and current open-market behavior. One approach is to read the open-market clearing price for the inventory each deal targets, then check that the deal floor sits at or above it and that the priority protects committed volume where the deal requires it.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements often carry both a negotiated deal and strong open-market demand, so a deal floor set below the open-market clearing price on those slots gives the deal buyer a discount on exactly the inventory that needed protecting. You may want to compare each premium-placement deal floor against the open-market clearing price for the same slot before treating the negotiated price as settled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, open-market clearing prices on product and category sections can rise with seasonal intent, so a deal floor negotiated off-season may sit below where the open market clears during a peak. You may want to recheck deal floors on high-intent sections against current open-market prices ahead of seasonal peaks rather than leaving the negotiated floor fixed.

learn_more.source_title: Deals in Prebid Server (deal priority and preferdeals)

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed configuration documentation for deal floors and line-item priority is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor and priority against the open-market clearing price for the inventory it targets, to see whether the deal and the open auction are aligned or undercutting each other.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the deals section of the Equativ console and list each active deal with its floor, its priority relative to the open auction, and the inventory it targets.
For each deal, note whether it is a Private Marketplace (PMP) deal meant to compete on price or a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal meant to deliver committed volume.
Record the priority setting for each deal, which decides whether a deal bid is preferred over a higher open-market bid or whether price alone wins.

equativ_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report of the open-market average clearing price for the inventory each deal targets, broken out by the same ad units or sections the deals cover.
Compare each deal floor against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory to see whether the floor sits above, at, or below it.
Note any case where a deal floor sits below the open-market clearing price, or where a Programmatic Guaranteed deal carries no priority over the open auction, which points to a misalignment to fix.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each deal's floor and priority alongside the open-market clearing price for the inventory it targets. Deals whose floors sit at or above the open-market clearing price, and whose priority protects committed volume where the deal requires it, pass. A deal floor sitting below the open-market clearing price for the same inventory, or a Programmatic Guaranteed deal set at the same priority as the open market so its committed volume can be outbid, is a fail recorded with the deal ID and the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor and priority against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that deal floors and priority at {{site_name}} are not aligned with the open market, so deals and the open auction can undercut each other. A deal floor set below where the inventory already clears in the open auction lets the negotiated buyer win at an unintended discount, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal set at the same priority as the open market can have its committed volume outbid. {{voice_recommend}} reading the open-market clearing price for the inventory each deal targets, setting deal floors at or above that price, and giving committed deals priority over the open auction so they deliver the volume that was negotiated. After the change, recheck deal floors against open-market prices on a regular cadence so they keep pace with how the inventory clears.
